era_context: The early modern period in full swing: the Protestant Reformation was consolidating in Northern Europe while the Catholic Counter-Reformation was beginning to organize. The Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent was expanding aggressively in the Mediterranean and Eastern Europe. In the Americas, Spanish conquistadors were consolidating control over newly conquered territories. The Ming Dynasty in China was in the reign of the Jiajing Emperor, facing increasing pressure from Mongol and Jurchen raids on the northern frontier.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands beyond Spanish contact zones
Southeast Asian interior kingdoms (limited European knowledge)

events.title: Spanish capture of La Goletta (Halq al-Wadi)
events.description: Spanish forces under Charles V captured the fortress of La Goletta (Halq al-Wadi) on the Tunisian coast. The fortress was a strategic stronghold controlling access to the Mediterranean and the port of Tunis. The Spanish conquest was part of the broader Habsburg effort to contain Ottoman expansion in the Mediterranean and to establish European control over North African trade routes. The capture of La Goletta was a significant military achievement and demonstrated Spanish naval and military capability. The fortress would remain under Spanish control until 1574, when it was recaptured by the Ottomans.
events.certainty_note: The capture of La Goletta in 1535 is well-documented in Spanish, European, and Ottoman sources. The date and major events are firmly established.

events.title: Henry VIII breaks with Rome; Act of Supremacy
events.description: King Henry VIII formally established himself as Supreme Head of the Church of England through the Act of Supremacy, completing the break with the Roman Catholic Church that had begun with his marriage to Anne Boleyn in 1533. The act declared that the monarch, not the Pope, held ultimate authority over the English church. This religious and political transformation had profound consequences for English society, including the dissolution of monasteries, the suppression of Catholic practices, and the establishment of a Protestant ecclesiastical hierarchy. The break with Rome was driven by Henry's desire for an annulment from Catherine of Aragon and his determination to assert royal supremacy over all institutions within his realm.

sources.name: Letters and Papers, Foreign and Domestic, of the Reign of Henry VIII

events.certainty_note: The Act of Supremacy is precisely dated to 1535 in English statutory records. The formal break with Rome is extensively documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Reformation in Sweden
events.description: King Gustav I Vasa of Sweden formally established Lutheranism as the state religion, completing the Swedish Reformation. The process involved the confiscation of Catholic Church properties, the establishment of a Lutheran ecclesiastical hierarchy, and the suppression of Catholic practices. The Swedish Reformation was driven by both religious conviction and the Crown's desire to consolidate power and acquire church wealth. The transformation aligned Sweden with other Scandinavian and Northern European Protestant states and had lasting consequences for Swedish culture, politics, and society.

sources.name: Swedish royal decrees and ecclesiastical records

events.certainty_note: The formal establishment of Lutheranism in Sweden is attested in Swedish sources, though the process was gradual and some sources date key events to 1536 or 1537. The year 1535 represents a significant milestone in the reformation process.

events.title: Cartier's first voyage to North America
events.description: French explorer Jacques Cartier undertook his first voyage to North America, sailing along the coast of present-day Newfoundland, Labrador, and the Gulf of St. Lawrence. Cartier made contact with Indigenous peoples, including the Mi'kmaq and Iroquoian-speaking groups, and established French territorial claims in North America. The voyage resulted in the kidnapping of two Indigenous youths, Domagaia and Taignoagny, who would serve as interpreters on Cartier's subsequent voyages. Cartier's explorations provided France with detailed geographical knowledge and established the foundation for French colonial claims in North America.

sources.name: Cartier's own account of the voyage (Brief recit et succincte narration)

events.certainty_note: Cartier's first voyage is documented in his own detailed account and French royal records. The dates and major events are well-established.

events.title: Founding of Lima by Francisco Pizarro
events.description: Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro founded the city of Lima (Ciudad de los Reyes) as the capital of Spanish Peru. The city was established on the coast of the Pacific Ocean, providing a strategic port for Spanish colonial administration and trade. Lima quickly became the administrative and economic center of Spanish South America, and it would eventually become one of the most important cities in the Spanish colonial empire. The founding of Lima represented the consolidation of Spanish control over the former Inca Empire and the establishment of permanent European settlement in the region.

sources.name: Spanish chronicles and accounts of Pizarro's conquest (Cieza de León, Betanzos)
Contemporary Spanish royal records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The founding of Lima in 1535 is documented in Spanish sources and is a well-established date in colonial history.

events.title: Mughal Empire under Akbar's father Humayun faces crisis
events.description: The Mughal Empire under Humayun faced a severe political and military crisis in 1535. Humayun's brother Kamran Mirza rebelled against his authority, and the Afghan Sher Khan (later Sher Shah Suri) launched a major military offensive against Mughal forces. These challenges to Humayun's rule would eventually lead to his temporary loss of power and exile. The crisis of 1535 marked the beginning of a period of instability in the Mughal Empire that would last until Akbar's consolidation of power in the 1560s. The period saw significant military campaigns, territorial losses, and political upheaval.
key_figures: Sher Khan/Sher Shah Suri (Afghan military leader)

sources.name: Mughal chronicles and official histories (Akbarnama by Abul Fazl)

events.certainty_note: The political crisis of 1535 is attested in Mughal chronicles, though precise dating of events is sometimes uncertain. The year 1535 marks the beginning of a period of instability.

events.title: Menno Simons founds Anabaptist movement
events.description: Menno Simons, a former Catholic priest, formally broke with the Catholic Church and became a leader of the Anabaptist movement in Northern Europe. Simons advocated for adult baptism, pacifism, and the separation of church and state—radical positions that distinguished Anabaptists from both Catholics and mainstream Protestants. The Anabaptist movement, which Simons helped to organize and lead, faced severe persecution from both Catholic and Protestant authorities. Despite persecution, the movement grew and eventually established communities in the Netherlands, Germany, and later in North America. Menno Simons' leadership was crucial in shaping the theological and organizational character of Anabaptism.

sources.name: Menno Simons' own writings and theological works

events.certainty_note: Menno Simons' break with the Catholic Church and emergence as an Anabaptist leader occurred around 1535–1536. The precise date is somewhat uncertain, but 1535 is commonly cited.
cross_references: 1536-001 (Execution of Anne Boleyn, contemporary religious upheaval)

events.title: Consolidation of Inca civil war; Atahualpa's dominance
events.description: By 1535, Atahualpa had consolidated his control over the Inca Empire following his victory over his half-brother Huáscar in the civil war. However, Atahualpa's rule was unstable and faced challenges from various factions within the Inca nobility. The civil war had weakened the empire's military and administrative structures, making it vulnerable to Spanish conquest. Atahualpa's consolidation of power was short-lived, as Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro was preparing his invasion and would capture and execute Atahualpa in 1533 (some sources date this to 1535).

sources.name: Spanish chronicles by Pizarro's companions (Cieza de León, Betanzos, Cobo)
Quipucamayoc (Inca oral tradition recorded by Spanish)

events.certainty_note: The dating of events in the Inca civil war is uncertain, with sources varying on whether Atahualpa's consolidation occurred in 1533, 1535, or 1536. The year 1535 is approximate.
cross_references: 1533-001 (Execution of Atahualpa, though dating is disputed)

events.title: Ottoman-Safavid War (Safavid-Ottoman conflict)
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent and the Safavid Persian Empire under Shah Tahmasp engaged in military conflict over control of Mesopotamia and the Caucasus region. The conflict was part of a broader rivalry between the two Islamic empires for regional hegemony. Ottoman forces captured Baghdad in 1534 and continued military operations in 1535. The Ottoman-Safavid wars would continue intermittently for centuries, with both empires competing for control of strategic territories and trade routes. The conflict had significant consequences for the peoples of the region and shaped the geopolitical landscape of the Middle East.
events.certainty_note: The Ottoman-Safavid conflict of 1535 is documented in Ottoman and Persian sources. The capture of Baghdad in 1534 and subsequent operations in 1535 are well-attested.
cross_references: 1555-002 (Peace of Amasya between Ottoman and Safavid empires)

events.title: Valor Ecclesiasticus survey in England
events.description: The Valor Ecclesiasticus, a comprehensive survey of the wealth and property of the English Church, was completed in 1535. The survey was commissioned by Henry VIII's government under Thomas Cromwell to assess the extent of ecclesiastical wealth and to facilitate the subsequent dissolution of monasteries. The Valor Ecclesiasticus provided detailed information about monastic lands, revenues, and assets, making it an invaluable historical document for understanding the medieval English church. The survey's completion marked a crucial step in the Crown's plan to seize church property and consolidate royal power.

sources.name: Letters and Papers, Foreign and Domestic, of the Reign of Henry VIII

events.certainty_note: The Valor Ecclesiasticus is precisely dated to 1535 and survives as a primary historical document. The survey's completion is well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Act of Supremacy is sometimes given as 1534 rather than 1535, depending on the source and whether one counts the parliamentary session or the formal enactment. Modern scholarship has questioned the traditional narrative of Henry VIII's religious motivations, suggesting that political and dynastic concerns were more important than genuine theological conviction. The Anabaptist movement's origins are complex, and attributing its founding solely to Menno Simons in 1535 oversimplifies a more gradual process of religious radicalization. The dating of Atahualpa's consolidation of power in the Inca civil war is disputed, with sources varying on whether key events occurred in 1533, 1535, or 1536.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1535 is rich for European events, particularly England, where government records and diplomatic correspondence are extensive. Ottoman and Persian sources provide good coverage of the Ottoman-Safavid conflict. However, sources are heavily weighted toward European and Ottoman perspectives; Indigenous American accounts are mediated through Spanish chroniclers. The Valor Ecclesiasticus is an exceptionally detailed primary source that provides rare quantitative data about medieval institutions. The historical record for 1535 reflects the biases of surviving documentation: court-sponsored records, diplomatic correspondence, and later compilations dominate. Indigenous perspectives, particularly from the Americas, are underrepresented and filtered through colonial-era sources.

graph_edges.note: The capture of La Goletta in 1535 was followed by the Sack of Tunis in 1536 as part of the same campaign
The Act of Supremacy of 1535 provided the legal basis for the dissolution of monasteries beginning in 1536
Cartier's first voyage in 1535 was followed by his second voyage in 1536
The founding of Lima in 1535 occurred during the Inca civil war
The Valor Ecclesiasticus survey of 1535 directly enabled the dissolution of monasteries in 1536
